title: podman pull rejects container signature
body:
### Issue Description

I want to pull a multiarch container from a mirrored repository which was signed in the original repo. The policy.json was adjusted accordingly to verify signatures. The pull fails with : Source image rejected: Signature for identity not accepted

I think that there maybe a bug in the policy evaluation in the remap feature, see policy below. I double check my policies, and it seems there is no error in the policy.  
Or is there a different way to trust signatures created in a different repo ?



### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue

1.
The container was originally created, pushed and signed as `cr.example.com/group1/subgroup1/repo1/debian:bookworm-testing`
`cosign sign --tlog-upload=false --recursive --key private.key cr.example.com/group1/subgroup1/repo1/debian:bookworm-testing`

2.
Then I copy the container including its signatures and attestments to a different repo in the same registry
`cosign copy --force cr.example.com/group1/subgroup1/repo1/debian:bookworm-testing cr.example.com/group2/repo2/debian:bookworm-testing`

3.
Now I want to pull the container including signature checks from the new rppo:
```
podman pull cr.example.com/group2/repo2/debian:bookworm-testing

Trying to pull cr.example.com/group2/repo2/debian:bookworm-testing...
Error: copying system image from manifest list: Source image rejected: Signature for identity "cr.example.com/group1/subgroup1/repo1/debian" is not accepted
```

### Describe the results you received

Error: copying system image from manifest list: Source image rejected: Signature for identity "cr.example.com/group1/subgroup1/repo1/debian" is not accepted

### Describe the results you expected

podman pull successfully pulls the container although the signature referrers to a different repository with policy used correctly

### podman info output

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

None

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

The trust policies are describes with signedIdentity -> remapIdentity in /etc/containers/policy.json
```
{
    "default": [
        {
            "type": "insecureAcceptAnything"
        }
    ],
    "transports": {
        "docker": {
            "cr.example.com/group1/subgroup1/repo1/debian": [
                {
                    "type": "sigstoreSigned",
                    "keyPath": "/etc/containers/signing-key.pem",
                    "signedIdentity": {
                        "type": "matchRepository"
                    }
                }
            ],
            " cr.example.com/group2/repo2/debian": [
                {
                    "type": "sigstoreSigned",
                    "keyPath": "/etc/containers/signing-key.pem",
                    "signedIdentity": {
                        "type": "remapIdentity",
                        "prefix": " cr.example.com/group2/repo2",
                        "signedPrefix": "cr.example.com/group1/subgroup1/repo1"
                    }
                }
            ]
        }
    }
}
```

/etc/containers/registries.d/reg.yaml
```
docker:
    cr.example.com:
        use-sigstore-attachments: true
```

comments.body:
> ```
> cr.example.com/group2/repo2/debian:bookworm-testing...
> Signature for identity "cr.example.com/group1/subgroup1/repo1/debian" is not accepted
> ```

The signature identity is missing the `:bookworm-testing` tag. `remapIdentity` has no equivalent of `matchRepository` (and that policy is weak, so I’d prefer not to add it to `remapIdentity`).

Make the policy work for the original repo without `matchRepository`; if you are using `cosign`, IIRC that is  `--sign-identity $repo:$tag`, and also `--recursive` is needed.

thanks, after signing the container with the additional cosign parameter `--sign-container-identity` the `remapIdentity` policy works. The issue here was i guess, that i signed the image identified by a sh256, not by a tag name.

The documentation does not say anything about any wildcard syntax in that position.

The above is not a clear example of your desired outcome; guessing, just dropping the `/*` part might work.

If the goal is to support arbitrary combinations of repos in references vs. signatures (e.g. so that …/debian signatures are accepted for …/arch references), that’s not a feature and you’d need to list the accepted combinations individually.

okay, sorry, i try to explain it more detailed. i have multiple repos, in each of them i  build multiple container
eg:

- repo1:
  - cr.example.com/group1/subgroup1/repo1/debian:latest
  - cr.example.com/group1/subgroup1/repo1/debian:bookworm
- repo2:
  - cr.example.com/group1/subgroup1/repo2/ubuntu:latest
- repo3: 
  - cr.example.com/group1/subgroup1/repo3/arch:latest
-repo4:
  - cr.example.com/group1/subgroup1/repo4/jre:16
  - cr.example.com/group1/subgroup1/repo4/jre:17
  - cr.example.com/group1/subgroup1/repo4/jre:18

all these different container form different repos end in a single mirror registry of an independent repo: cr.example.com/group2/repo2

- cr.example.com/group2/repo2/debian:latest
- cr.example.com/group2/repo2/debian:bookworm
- cr.example.com/group2/repo2/ubuntu:latest
- ...
- cr.example.com/group2/repo2/jre:17
- cr.example.com/group2/repo2/jre:18

all container are sigened in their respective development repos and then copied including their signatures to the mirror repo.
as i learned before to do this i need to specify the `sign-container-identity` and then remap the identities so that the original signature is still valid and accepted although the container now reside in a repo where they where not signed for.

so i was wondering if there is a way to configure the policy.json in a general way to accept all signatures from  cr.example.com/group1/subgroup1/* within cr.example.com/group2

just dropping the `/*` part from my above example sadly does not work. No, i don t want that …/debian is accepted by …/arch reference. I would like that podman accepts an image pulled from `cr.example.com/group2/repo2/debian:latest` althougt it was signed in ` cr.example.com/group1/subgroup1/repo1/debian:latest`, without specifying each possible combination with a `remapIdentity` in the policy file. 

Sorry, i hope this clarified my problem.  Thanks!

Thanks for the clarification.

_Alternatives_ are (~deliberately) not supported. So this would need to be an explicit `remapIdentity` for `…group2/repo2/debian` → `…/group1/subgroup1/repo1/debian`, and so on for every repo (at a repo level, not at a tag level, as long as every repo only has one origin).

Alternatively, if this is a “staging / testing” setup where the group1 repos _know_ the goal is to deliver in group2, and group2 is the “more public one”, whereas group1 are ~internal, the group1 repos can all use `--sign-container-identity` with the final `group2` identities; and while testing, use `remapIdentity` to access the staging group1 environment (and no `remapIdentity` to access images in the “final” group2 environment).

>  (and no remapIdentity to access images in the “final” group2 environment).

This is, BTW a benefit / a recommended workflow: for staging / pre-production testing, use `remapIdentity`, but design it so that ~external consumers of the published images can just use `sigstoreSigned` without any remapping (and they don’t see any internal hostnames).
